system_context.business_goals: Registration of data concerning courses and students.

architectures.name: MISOC2000 Architecture
architectures.description: A layered, client-server architecture for registering course and student data across 15 Dutch army training centers. It is divided into macro (environmental) and micro (internal) architectures, with the micro architecture organized into functional subsystems and a three-tier structure (client, server, database).
architectures.architecture_characteristics: Modularity, Maintainability, Interoperability (with external systems like KIO), Centralized Data Management, Role-based Access Control

key_components.responsibilities: Formulating course catalogs and production plans.
Distribution of course catalogs and recording agreements with customers.
Management of the availability of locations and items.
Exporting cost information to KIO and importing information about cost centers from KIO.
An extension of the P-module to record personnel information specific for training centers.
Administrative purposes, like maintenance of authorization and configuration data.
Logs users in to and out of MISOC2000; registers user with database.
Hosts the core business logic for all subsystems (Product, Sales, Student, etc.) and mediates access to the central database.

architectures.data_flow: Users authenticate via the Authentication Client (C-09), which validates credentials against the General module (C-08) and database. Upon successful login, users interact with client applications that communicate with the central Core Data & Business Logic Server (C-10). This server processes requests from functional subsystems (e.g., Student registration, Course Programming) and performs all create, read, update, delete (CRUD) operations on the central database. Data flows bi-directionally between the server and external systems: the Economics module (C-06) exchanges cost data with the external KIO system, and the Personnel module (C-07) extends an external P-module. Most business processes (e.g., creating a course, enrolling a student) involve sequential interactions between multiple subsystems via the central server.
architectural_constraints: Systems owned by training centers can be changed autonomously; those owned by others (e.g., KIO, P-module) require coordination.
Client and server machine configurations must adhere to the LAN2000 standard.
Architecture is built around a centralized, shared database.
architectures.notes: The architecture is highly integrated and centralized. The 'Student' component is a central hub with dependencies on most other functional modules, indicating it is critical for core operations. Changes to external interfaces (KIO, P-module) or the core server/database are complex and carry high risk. The layered and modular design supports maintainability within the bounds of the central server, but scaling individual functions independently is difficult.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: A user at any of the 15 training centers must be able to log in via the Authentication Client and create a new student record in the system, entering data such as name, identification number, and assigned course, which is then stored via the Core Data & Business Logic Server.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match. The layered client-server architecture with dedicated Student and Authentication components supports this core registration function.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: This is the fundamental, daily task for personnel managing training programs; system failure here would halt core operations.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system must allow authorized users to register a new course offering, defining its code, title, duration, associated costs (Economics), required personnel (Personnel), and logistical resources (Logistics), with all data persisted by the Core Data & Business Logic Server.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match. The architecture's functional subsystems (Product, Economics, Personnel, Logistics) are designed to handle these distinct aspects of course data.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Comprehensive course setup is essential for planning and delivering army training across all centers.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer must be able to modify the business logic for calculating course fees within the Economics subsystem without requiring changes to the user interface in the Client tier or the data schema in the Database tier.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match, assuming clean separation of tiers. The three-tier structure should allow logic changes in the server's business layer to be isolated.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Pricing policies change; maintaining modularity reduces development time and risk for updates.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system must support the addition of a 16th training center within one month, involving deploying the client application to the new location and ensuring it can authenticate and communicate with the existing Core Data & Business Logic Server without performance degradation for the original 15 centers.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Likely requires indirect changes. While client-server architecture supports new clients, scalability of the central server (load, network configuration) must be evaluated and may need enhancement.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: The army's training footprint may expand; the system must scale operationally with minimal disruption.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer must be able to apply a security patch to the Core Data & Business Logic Server during a scheduled 2-hour maintenance window on a weekend without requiring updates to the client applications at the 15 distributed centers.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match, assuming backward-compatible interfaces. The layered separation should allow independent server maintenance.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Regular maintenance and security updates are critical for a military system, and they must be performed with minimal downtime and coordination overhead.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system should provide an exploratory API from the Core Data & Business Logic Server to allow external reporting tools to generate ad-hoc analytics on student completion rates across all courses and centers, without direct database access.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect change required. This would necessitate designing and exposing a new API layer on the server, which is not described in the current component list.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Enhanced data analysis could improve training program effectiveness and resource planning, though not an immediate operational requirement.

scenario_classification.rationale: The current architecture explicitly includes an Authentication Client (C-09) for login, a Student component (C-03) for registering student information, and a Core Data & Business Logic Server (C-10) for processing and storing data. The three-tier client-server structure and defined data flow directly support this scenario.

The architecture includes dedicated functional subsystems: Product (C-01) for course catalogs, Economics (C-06) for costs, Personnel (C-07) for personnel data, and Logistics (C-05) for resources. All interact via the Core Data & Business Logic Server (C-10) for data persistence, aligning perfectly with the scenario.

The three-tier architecture (client, server, database) promotes separation of concerns. Business logic for fee calculation resides in the Economics subsystem (C-06) on the server tier (C-10). Changes here should be isolated from the client UI and database schema, assuming well-defined interfaces.

While client-server architecture supports adding new clients, the scenario requires ensuring no performance degradation for existing centers. The centralized server (C-10) and database may face increased load, network latency, and configuration needs (LAN2000 standard). Scalability of the central server is not guaranteed without evaluation and potential enhancements.

The layered separation allows independent maintenance of the server tier. As long as the server's interfaces remain backward-compatible, applying a security patch to the Core Data & Business Logic Server (C-10) should not require client updates, supporting scheduled maintenance.

The current architecture does not describe an API layer for external reporting tools. Providing ad-hoc analytics without direct database access requires designing and exposing a new API on the server (C-10), involving Student (C-03) and Product (C-01) data, which is not an existing capability.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required. The architecture's defined components and data flow directly support user authentication, student record creation, and data storage via the central server.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Not applicable; scenario is directly supported.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required. The functional subsystems (Product, Economics, Personnel, Logistics) and central server are designed to handle comprehensive course registration.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Not applicable; scenario is directly supported.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No architectural changes required. Modifying business logic in the Economics subsystem should be isolated due to the three-tier separation, assuming clean interfaces.
issues_identified: Assumes well-defined interfaces between tiers; tight coupling could necessitate client or database changes.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: If coupling exists, refactor to enforce separation of concerns within the server tier.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Requires scalability assessment and potential enhancements: server load balancing, database performance tuning, network configuration for LAN2000 compliance at the new center, and possible client deployment automation.
issues_identified: Centralized server and database may become bottlenecks; network latency could affect performance; coordination needed for LAN2000 standard compliance.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Deploy a read-only replica of the database at the new center for certain queries, or implement caching mechanisms on the server to reduce load. However, these still require architectural changes.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No architectural changes required. The layered design allows independent server maintenance, assuming backward-compatible interfaces.
issues_identified: Assumes patch is backward-compatible; if not, client updates may be needed, violating the scenario.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: If interfaces change, maintain versioned APIs to support old clients during transition.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Design and implement a new API layer on the Core Data & Business Logic Server to expose data for external reporting tools. This includes defining endpoints, authentication/authorization, data aggregation logic for analytics, and ensuring performance does not impact core operations.
issues_identified: Introducing a new API increases attack surface; performance overhead on server; may require changes to business logic to support complex queries; need to maintain data consistency and security.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use existing database reporting tools with controlled, read-only access via a dedicated reporting database replica, but this violates the 'without direct database access' requirement and may pose security risks.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: C-10 is the central server component, impacted by all six scenarios. It is critical for core operations like authentication, course registration, business logic, scalability, maintenance, and external reporting. Failure to address its interactions risks system-wide bottlenecks (S-04), security vulnerabilities (S-06), and operational disruptions across all stakeholder roles. Its centrality means issues here propagate widely, making it the highest-priority hotspot.

C-03 is involved in user authentication (S-01) and external reporting (S-06). While S-01 is low-risk, S-06 introduces medium risk due to new API requirements that could affect authentication security and performance. Not addressing this could compromise data security for external access and impact end-users and administrators managing access controls.

C-09 supports authentication (S-01) and scalability for a new center (S-04). S-04 poses medium risk due to potential bottlenecks and LAN2000 compliance issues. Neglecting this could degrade performance for end-users at the new center and increase administrative overhead for network and server management.

C-01 is part of the Product subsystem for course registration (S-02) and external reporting (S-06). S-02 is low-risk, but S-06 requires new APIs that may affect registration data exposure. Not addressing could lead to inconsistent data in reports, impacting end-user decision-making and requiring developer effort to fix integrations.

C-06 is in the Economics subsystem for course registration (S-02) and business logic changes (S-03). Both scenarios are low-risk with direct support, but S-03 notes a risk of tight coupling. If not addressed, it could lead to maintenance challenges for developers and minor disruptions for end-users during updates, but overall impact is limited.

architectural_strengths: Strength 1: The architecture's three-tier separation (client, server, database) and modular functional subsystems (Product, Economics, Personnel, Logistics) enable direct support for core business operations with low risk and effort. Evidence: Scenarios S-01 (user authentication and record creation), S-02 (comprehensive course registration), and S-05 (server maintenance) are classified as 'Direct' with no architectural changes required, low estimated effort, and low risk levels.

Strength 2: The centralized Core Data & Business Logic Server (C-10) provides a cohesive layer for business logic and data management, simplifying core operations and maintenance. Evidence: C-10 is involved in all six scenarios, demonstrating its central role in supporting authentication, registration, business logic updates, and maintenance, with direct support in S-01, S-02, S-03, and S-05 requiring minimal changes.
architectural_weaknesses: Weakness 1: The architecture's centralized server and database design creates scalability and performance bottlenecks, particularly for expansion scenarios. Evidence: S-04 (adding a new center) is classified as 'Indirect' with widespread changes, medium effort, and medium risk due to potential server/database bottlenecks and network latency issues, indicating limited horizontal scalability.

Weakness 2: The architecture lacks built-in support for secure external data access without direct database exposure, increasing security and performance risks. Evidence: S-06 (external reporting) is 'Indirect' with localized changes, medium effort, and medium risk, requiring a new API layer that increases attack surface and may impact core server performance.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture demonstrates good modifiability for isolated, low-impact changes within its tiered structure (e.g., business logic updates in S-03), but struggles with cross-cutting concerns like scalability (S-04) and new integration points (S-06), revealing rigidity in its centralized components and limited extensibility for external access.

recommendations.recommendation: Refactor the Core Data & Business Logic Server (C-10) to implement a microservices or modular monolith pattern, separating authentication, business logic, and reporting into independently scalable services with well-defined APIs.
recommendations.rationale: This improves modifiability by decoupling core functions, allowing targeted scaling for high-demand services (e.g., reporting APIs in S-06) and reducing bottlenecks during expansion (S-04), while maintaining the layered separation that supports direct scenarios.

recommendations.recommendation: Introduce a dedicated, versioned API gateway layer for all external access (including future reporting needs in S-06) with built-in authentication, rate limiting, and caching to secure and offload the central server.
recommendations.rationale: This enhances modifiability by centralizing integration concerns, reducing the attack surface and performance impact on C-10, and providing a flexible entry point for new external requirements without altering core business logic.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement database read replicas and server-side caching mechanisms to support scalability for the new center (S-04) and improve performance for reporting queries (S-06).
recommendations.rationale: This improves modifiability by allowing horizontal scaling of data access without immediate re-architecture, addressing bottlenecks identified in S-04 and S-06 with manageable changes to the data layer.

recommendations.recommendation: Formalize interface contracts between the server tier and subsystems (e.g., Economics subsystem C-06) to enforce separation of concerns and prevent tight coupling noted in S-03.
recommendations.rationale: This strengthens modifiability by ensuring business logic changes remain isolated, reducing the risk of unintended side effects and supporting the architecture's existing strength in direct support scenarios.
